speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five, interview with Dr. Andreas Chrambach. I asked you about your personal emotional reaction to the day of liberation, if you can describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean my emotional reaction to liberation that has lasted me for the rest of my life was the exuberance [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of being alive, of somebody that had no hope at all of ever coming out alive, somebody who was with great certainty sentenced to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in some ways I returned from the other side back to life at age 17. And this has given life a quality of-- of rapture, of enthusiasm that has really never left me, you know, the joy of being alive every day. So I think that-- that's really the-- the main-- that's-- that's the only emotional effect the liberation had. You know, obviously, there are a lot of intellectual effects, you know? One of those was political. I mean just the-- the strong conviction [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the direction toward humanism, in the direction of avoiding the inhumanity of man to man that had brought me and Germany and the world into that sort of situation, as I'd experienced.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I remember from the very beginnings after liberation, I was strongly opposed to retaliation, in contradistinction to practically everybody else I was liberated with. In fact, the first couple they sighted after liberation, was shot by liberated prisoners. Now, I personally never felt, and of course it also has to do with my religiosity of this time that-- that one had to take the opposite viewpoint, and the humanist viewpoint. But I've been, I think, ever since passionate in-- in humanist politics. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And does that extend even to the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I felt very strongly that-- that you had to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] suddenly, at that time, I felt very strongly that I had to be won over by humanism, and not fortified in their inhuman positions by retaliation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think over the years, I'm not sure that this has not changed. My wife asked me the question the other day, in conjunction with the Bosnian conflict, what I would do if I could ever meet the chap who killed my brother and I was fully armed and he was not. And I must confess, I probably would shoot him. But-- but that sort of hardening of one's philosophy, I think, has happened to me over a lifetime, increasingly. And I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the weight of the loss, particularly of my brother, has-- has increased with time, rather than decreased. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the thoughts of-- you had no idea where your-- where your mother was, where your father was. On the day of liberation, did you give some thought to-- to-- to the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, of course, I had no idea. I mean, we had an arrangement to meet again if you should survive in Budapest, or with our relatives in Dresden. That was the arrangement. And so it was for that reason that with a great deal of hope, I went back to Budapest as soon as the Red Army allowed us to travel. It was-- it was a month later. In April '45, we were issued documents saying that we were liberated by the Red Army in [INAUDIBLE]. And they asked all these Red Army authorities to give us all the help they could to get to our points of destination, and armed us in documents like this. I made it, in fact, in April '45 back to-- to Budapest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I then waited. You see, I waited to hear from my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: But physically, where did you go in Budapest when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to my French teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My French teacher was a lifelong communist, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] obviously, nobody knew that in the German school. In fact, he took credit, I think, for arranging this early high school graduation that I was talking about for the people of Jewish background. But whether or not it's like this, he was a-- he was a family man with two children himself. He took me in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I stayed with him and his family from April of '45 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the fall of '45. These were still times of extreme insecurity in Budapest. I mean, as the young male that I was, I couldn't really cross the bridges between Buda and Pest without being picked up by some Red Army patrol, and sent to Siberia for 10 or 20 years of work. They picked up everybody. And if it wasn't Siberia, it was some horrible other place in Hungary. Anyway, people were picked up in the streets when they looked like good work-- work horses. Women were raped in an incredible degree so that in the street where I lived they had installed an alarm system, installing cow bells in every house. A woman was raped in house one. Then everybody else started to ring these bells until the-- the-- the-- the Army police came. So in a few ways, people protected themselves. And it was really a lawless period. And also I had nothing to eat, really. I mean I-- I-- I-- I had no money. Our house was destroyed. I picked up a few of the things that weren't destroyed, like my father's hunting trophies, and sold them to some restaurant. But by the fall, it was clear I couldn't survive. So, I found a job in a-- in a village, supervising a wood-cutting crew, simply because I knew mathematics. I could calculate the amount of wood they were cutting every day, and make sure that they didn't steal extravagant amounts of his wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that kept me in bread and-- not butter, but in bread for the winter of '45, '46. And during that time I received the Red Cross-- from the International Red Cross in Geneva, a note from my mother saying that she had survived and was staying with my relatives in Dresden. On hearing that, then I got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] travel papers from the Red Army. And arrived in-- in January '46. January '46, I arrived in Dresden, where obviously, no one had an idea that I had survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just walked in, and was reunited with my mother, and have really stayed-- then stayed with her for a long time. I mean, I took a job in Dresden. Then I moved to Berlin, went to the University of Berlin, moved her to Berlin to live with me. We left Germany in '48 for England, and then in '49 for America together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You may ask why? Maybe I should talk a little bit about that. If I could ask you this-- how did you find your mother in terms of her health when you arrived in Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, her health-- her health was-- was not worrisome. I mean, she was emaciated like everybody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was spiritually intact, I think, largely because of her very strong faith, religious faith, and physically, not worse off than most of us. I mean she had worked in a-- she had been in Bergen-Belsen, and then farmed out from Bergen-Belsen camp into a munitions factory, and been liberated in the-- in the factory by Americans. And then made it afoot to Saxony, and Dresden, and stayed there with my relatives. I mean these were-- OK. So she was-- she was in good shape. Of course, you know, both she and I were sort of waiting on a daily basis to hear from my father and my brother for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a point when you received confirmation that they were not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I mean, you know after-- after everybody was repatriated a year later, or two years later, you know, and nothing was heard from them, and I got information about my brother being registered as a Hungarian translator in Sachsenhausen and Buchenwald. That's one, one-- as far as my brother was concerned. So we knew he was in Buchenwald when the war was over. Well, if two years later he didn't show up, he was obviously dead. That's all I know. And similar thing for my father. I mean, I have an oral report of somebody who knew him in Hungary, and was in Mauthauson with him, and still saw him days before liberation. And he was so weak he could hardly stand up anymore. So he obviously, whether he died prior to liberation or a few days afterwards, I don't know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicated then that your next move was to London, or to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I mean, I should say, you know, which is interesting, you know I went back to Berlin. And as the university opened in the fall of '46, I started studying law there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just spent two-plus years in Berlin, very enthusiastically. They were wonderful days for me, in spite of the fact there was relatively little to eat. But the climate, the political climate was one of enormous hopes. There was no state structure and people earnestly thought how they could build up a-- a humanist and-- and better new society, different from the old one. So they were very optimistic days, and full of the sort of enthusiasm that I felt myself about being alive. I mean, that's, I think, how most people felt. And the consequence, you know, was wonderful theater and music, in spite of the fact it was all done in pretty decrepit-looking buildings, and so on. And an enormous amount of camaraderie among the students, who shared everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And interesting, from our viewpoint, something that did bother me-- a great deal of philo-Semitism. I mean, I was made as sort of into a statue amongst students having come back from Auschwitz. I mean people, in fact, you know, were so overwhelmingly friendly that this friendliness separated you emotionally from everybody else. I couldn't have-- what I had really wanted was not to be noticed, just be a common citizen and that never worked out. I mean, and I think to the present day it is a problem in relation to all the people who have knowledge about that time, particularly Germans. You know, when they hear of just the fact that I ended the war in concentration camp and not at the front, you see, that is a dividing-- a dividing element that's not easily overcome. And that sort of leaves you lonely. And I felt that very strongly in Berlin. But otherwise, these were great times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now why in the hell did I come to America? Because I participated every summer in French, German-French friendship camps, student friendship camps that were held in the French zone. So to get there, later during the blockade of Berlin. What happened is that for the French military airplane, you were flown out into the-- the zone. And we were there. I was instrumental in organizing these meetings.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the last meeting was over in '48, Berlin was still blockaded. And I went to the German authorities in Frankfurt, and said, look I need permission to-- residence-- I need a residence permission for West Germany because I'm not going to go back to blockaded Berlin. You have to view this from the political narrow-mindedness of 1948. In 1948, everybody, everybody assumed that the Red Army was going to march into Paris next week, and certainly Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and the German authorities simply said, we're sorry. You have no relatives in West Germany. We can't give you residence permit but we will be happy to put you into a-- a refugee camp. So obviously, I told them, before I put myself into a refugee camp, I would rather-- I think I told them verbatim-- walk afoot to Kamchatka. And so there was no place for me to stay. So since that then turned out that I got no residence permit in West Germany, the next good idea was to-- to join my mother, who was very anxious to be with her-- with her old parents, age 90 to-- to 95, who were, at that time living in California. And I said, well to hell with it. I'll go to California with her, learn English, which was about the only language I hadn't learned at that time. And-- and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so-- so we went only to England, because it had German visas available, and ended up in California, in Stockton in 1949. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that, you know-- that is, of course, why I'm here. You know, the-- I got more and more entangled in personal relations, and professional training, and whatnot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You resumed your academic career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after a while. I started picking cherries and onions with the Mexican field workers in Stockton for $6 a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then graduated from this to obviously, we had to make a living. My mother washed floors in the local hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I found a job at Montgomery Ward in the shoe department, and became their super shoe salesman, simply due to the fact that, I guess if you sell shoes, you have to know what the inventory is. And I had no problem remembering where the shoes were and what kind they had. So-- so they loved me in Montgomery Ward. And that turned out to be very useful. And then in the evening, out of boredom, I went to school. But not speaking English, I had to take mathematics. So since it was the only class I took, I was their star student in that-- differential equations class and differential calculus. And so the professor got all enamored with me. And she drove me to Berkeley one day and said, you know, I have this kid who is a bloody genius. You need him. And so she-- she brought me into contact with Berkeley. Then from then on, I lived in Berkeley, in the co-op, which was a place for 500 guys, where we did all the work ourselves for $50 a month. Tuition was $45 a semester. And those $50, and the $45 I could easily earn during the summer by selling shoes at Montgomery Ward. They took me back every summer. That's how I went through undergraduate school. And then, of course, in graduate school, it becomes easier. You get scholarships and whatnot. So ultimately in '59 I got a PhD in biochemistry and have been involved in biochemical research ever since, first in an institute for muscle disease in New York, then at Johns Hopkins University, now, for 31 years at the National Institute of Health.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to describe your marital status and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my marital status has been in two stages. I married first a woman, a biochemist colleague of mine, who is-- who is a daughter of a German physicist, the kind of German physicist that was illegally shipped into this country to support the space effort, space physicist. And she's a splendid scientist herself. And we have two daughters, Monica and Carla, who are now adults, 30 and 28 years old, respectively. But that marriage didn't last, because-- ultimately because we-- we couldn't bring up our daughters in such a way that my wife could keep her profession and she's-- she's a-- and the professionalism was really the heart of her existence. So it soured because in those years when I was highly successful in doing research, it was about 20% of her talent. She sat at home cooking, wasting her enormous talent, and became very bitter about this and ultimately, also had political reasons. I mean, she became more and more conservative. I think I've been becoming, if anything, progressively radical as years go by. So we got divorced when the kids were young teenagers. And I remarried Birgit, whom you are going to meet any minute now, who is another PhD biochemist from the University of London, born in Austria, and who spent most of her life in London. But well, now equal number of year in London as here. Since 1977, she's in this country. I think we-- I discussed it with her last night. We did get married in 1980, but we have been living together, I think, since 1978, or something like this. So and we are very happy, very happy couple, with-- blessed with two sons. And the only other thing, I'm going to finish with to say that I can make the very fortunate statement, not only of being cheerfully alive, but also of having four children, who are all healthy and intelligent, which I consider a rare blessing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever resume the practice of Roman Catholicism as far as religion goes after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not after-- after I left, I mean after I discontinued it and became more and more rooted in atheism, I have not thought of resuming it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some final thoughts about the Holocaust as you look back, what are some of the lessons that you feel the younger generation should be aware of from your experience? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the main lesson as I see it, is to know that inhumanity of man to man is given to us, is sort of part of us, as a possibility every time and everywhere. I mean I look at German fascism simply as one of the many inhumanities in the course of history. And I think we must be aware of the fact that humanity is, by and large, still not sufficiently developed to control hatreds, egotism, maliciousness of the sort that-- that we have seen in the Holocaust. I certainly consider it a-- a universal problem for mankind, and in some ways, for every human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so very, very much for this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So to end it, I'd like to introduce my family. This is my wife, Birgit, Birgit An der Lan, to be precise, because she prefers to use her maiden name. And my brats, to the right, Adam; and to the left, Max. Adam is 15. Max is 11. And I won't tell you how old Birgit is but she will add a few words of her own.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say that I'm grateful that you came, and made this document. I think it's very important, for-- both for the community and for us as a family. And I wish you well for your further documentation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. So I think That's it. That's my father, Richard, during his days as an officer in the German Imperial Army during the first World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximate date?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 1916. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother and my father, about at the time of their marriage in Breslau, shortly after 1924. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My brother Max during our high school years in Budapest around 1942. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] My parents, my brother Max, and myself around 1936, in the hunting estate of my father's in the Tatra mountains in Slovakia. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My brother Max and myself, during our high school years in Budapest, around 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother to the right, I am to the left. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My brother, Max, and I in sailor suits, in Berlin, around 1937, accompanied by various cousins, dogs, and in the left background, my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My maternal grandparents in their home shortly before leaving Germany in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Betty [? Friedlander ?] and Max [? Friedlander. ?] My paternal grandparents, Margaret and Carl Chrambach at the time of their golden wedding anniversary in 1928 in Breslau. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The [? Feruza ?] prison of the SS in Budapest, in which I was incarcerated in the fall of 1944. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A compound of the Birkenau Concentration Camp of the kind in which I was imprisoned until January '45. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Typical inside of a block in Birkenau was the bunks to the left, and the chimney and heating channel in the center. Now these bunks were filled with hay. And at each layer they were occupied by two prisoners. All these pictures of Birkenau were taken in 1994, upon my revisit of the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a typical stone structure for Auschwitz 1 of the kind in which I was liberated by the Red Army on January 28, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My mother, Annalise, at the center, age 93, in Stockton, California, flanked by my sons, Max to the left, and Adam to the right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my wife's family. To the left, my wife, Birgit. Then in order from left to right-- excuse me-- her step-father, George; her mother, Laura; and her sister, Jackie, her younger sister, Jackie. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that, at the center is again, my mother on her 90th birthday. Flanked in the front by-- well, flanked by all of her four grandchildren. To the left front, Max; right front, Adam. Right behind him is Carla. And behind Max is Monica. And the two girls allied with their respective men behind them. [PAUSES FOR 40 SECONDS]
